questions.question: Why are node-disjoint paths particularly valuable for quantum purification?

options.text: They have independent errors, enabling effective purification

questions.explanation: Node-disjoint paths share no intermediate nodes, so their errors are independent. Purification works best when combining pairs with uncorrelated errors, as correlated errors cannot be purified away.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of least-loaded load balancing?

options.text: It dynamically adapts to current network conditions
It always uses the highest fidelity path

questions.explanation: Least-loaded balancing tracks current load on each path and directs new requests to the least utilized path. This dynamically adapts to changing conditions rather than following a fixed pattern.

questions.question: How does multi-path routing improve fault tolerance?

options.text: By providing backup paths when primary paths fail

questions.explanation: When one path fails, traffic can be automatically rerouted to backup paths. With multiple paths available, single failures don't cause complete connectivity loss.

questions.question: What is the difference between node-disjoint and link-disjoint paths?

options.text: Node-disjoint share no nodes; link-disjoint may share nodes but not links

questions.explanation: Node-disjoint paths share no intermediate nodes (and thus no links). Link-disjoint paths may share nodes but use different links between them. Node-disjoint provides stronger independence.

questions.question: In fidelity-weighted load balancing, which paths receive more traffic?

options.text: Paths with higher fidelity and capacity

questions.explanation: Fidelity-weighted balancing assigns probability proportional to fidelity × capacity. High-fidelity, high-capacity paths receive more traffic, ensuring quality-aware distribution.

questions.question: How can aggregate capacity across multiple paths be calculated?
questions.explanation: When using multiple paths simultaneously, the aggregate capacity is the sum of individual path capacities. Each path contributes independently to total throughput.

questions.question: Round-robin load balancing distributes requests equally regardless of path capacity.
questions.explanation: True. Round-robin simply cycles through paths in order, giving each path the same number of requests. This may underutilize high-capacity paths and overload low-capacity ones.
